tasks.prompt: Warm-up: Add a 'Build' stage to the Jenkinsfile that runs 'npm ci' on a Node.js app. Intentionally break it by removing 'package.json' temporarily, push, and observe Jenkins mark the build as FAILED and halt before reaching any subsequent stage. Then restore the file and verify the stage turns green.
tasks.explanation: A non-zero exit code from any 'sh' step immediately fails the containing stage and stops the entire pipeline — no further stages run. This is the 'fail fast' principle in action: there is no value in testing or packaging code that cannot even install its dependencies. By deliberately triggering the failure here, you confirm that the gate is real before relying on it in production.
hints: If 'npm ci' gives 'npm: command not found', the Jenkins agent does not have Node.js installed. Either install Node.js on the agent or use a Docker-based agent: replace 'agent any' with 'agent { docker { image "node:20-alpine" } }' to run the stage inside a Node container.
If you deleted package.json and the push does not trigger a build, check that the GitLab webhook is still active (Settings → Webhooks) and the branch matches.

tasks.prompt: Core practice: Add a 'Test' stage to the Jenkinsfile after the Build stage. Write a simple failing test in your app (a test that asserts a wrong expected value), push, and confirm Jenkins marks the test stage red and stops the pipeline before the 'Build Image' stage. Then fix the test, push again, and confirm all three stages go green.
tasks.explanation: The Test stage demonstrates the CI quality gate in practice. The pipeline stops at the first failing stage; 'Build Image' never runs when tests fail, which means a broken image can never reach the registry. This is the exact scenario described in the lecture: a developer changed a greeting string but forgot to update the assertion — the pipeline caught it immediately. The fix is in the test (or the code), not in weakening the pipeline.
hints: If 'npm test' exits 0 even with a failing assertion, check that 'node test.js' actually propagates the AssertionError exit code. Node exits with code 1 on an unhandled AssertionError by default — if you wrapped it in a try/catch and didn't re-throw, the exit code stays 0.

If you want to see Jenkins display test results graphically, replace the raw 'node test.js' runner with a framework like Mocha that produces JUnit XML reports, then add 'post { always { junit "reports/*.xml" } }' to the Test stage.

tasks.prompt: Core practice: Add the 'Build Image' stage to the Jenkinsfile. Tag the image with the Jenkins BUILD_NUMBER environment variable. Run the full pipeline (Checkout → Build → Test → Build Image) and confirm the image appears in 'docker images' on the Jenkins host, tagged with the build number.
tasks.explanation: BUILD_NUMBER is a built-in Jenkins environment variable that increments automatically with each build. Tagging with it instead of 'latest' gives each image a unique, ordered identifier, so you can always tell which build produced which image. In the next lecture you'll switch to the Git commit SHA for an even stronger traceability guarantee. Mounting '/var/run/docker.sock' into the Jenkins container lets the Jenkins process invoke the host's Docker daemon — a common and convenient setup for CI pipelines that need to build images.
hints: If 'docker build' fails with 'permission denied while trying to connect to Docker daemon socket', the Jenkins process cannot reach the Docker socket. Recreate the Jenkins container with the '-v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock' flag and install Docker CLI inside it.

If the image builds but 'docker images' shows it on your local machine and not on the Jenkins agent, you are running Jenkins locally — in that case the image is on the same machine. If using a remote agent, connect to that agent to verify.

tasks.explanation: Stage ordering in a CI pipeline is not cosmetic — it is the definition of what the quality gate protects. Building an image before running tests defeats the entire purpose of CI: it means untested, potentially broken code can produce a registry artifact. The 'fail fast' guarantee only holds if the dangerous outputs (images) come after the gating checks (tests). The 'latest' tag problem is equally practical: without a unique, immutable tag, rollback requires guesswork, and in a multi-build environment two concurrent builds can corrupt each other's tags.
hints: To visualise the problem: draw the stage order as a flow. Ask 'if Test fails, what has already been produced?' In the broken version, the answer is 'a Docker image' — and that is the problem.
To see the 'latest' tag risk in action: push two commits in rapid succession and watch both builds try to tag 'myapp:latest' — the second overwrites the first.
